source.title: A dual-reporter system for investigating and optimizing protein translation and folding in E. coli

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Heterologous protein expression in E. coli often leads to misfolding, aggregation, and degradation. Existing methods for screening protein expression and folding are low-throughput and cannot simultaneously monitor translation and folding at the single-cell level. The goal is to develop a dual-reporter system that enables high-throughput screening of mutant libraries by simultaneously assessing in vivo protein translation and folding.

section_gists.heading: A dual-reporter system for investigating and optimizing protein translation and folding in E. coli
section_gists.gist: The paper introduces a dual-reporter biosensor system for simultaneous monitoring of protein translation and folding in E. coli. The system aims to enable rapid screening of mutant libraries. It was validated on five proteins, showing excellent correlation between reporter signals and protein expression/solubility. The system is applied in deep mutational scanning to select variants with high expression and altered stability. NGS analysis shows correlation between predicted and experimental stabilities, and mutational data aids protein structure calculations. The introduction highlights the need for efficient methods to improve heterologous protein expression, discussing strategies like codon optimization, solubility tags, and chaperone co-expression, but notes the lack of single-cell simultaneous monitoring.
process_signals: Need for high-throughput screening of mutant libraries
Desire to simultaneously monitor translation and folding at single-cell level
Existing methods are time-consuming and not amenable to large libraries
metrics: Correlation between reporter signals and protein expression/solubility
Correlation between predicted and experimental stabilities
failures_or_limits: No existing system enables simultaneous monitoring of translation and folding at single cell level

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist:
The dual-reporter system consists of a translation sensor (translation-coupling cassette with mCherry) and a folding sensor (IbpA promoter driving GFP). Translation sensor: correct translation unfolds mRNA secondary structure, exposing RBS for mCherry, so mCherry fluorescence is proportional to target protein expression. Folding sensor: misfolded proteins release RpoH, which induces IbpA promoter, leading to GFP expression. Tested effect of plasmid backbone (pBBR1 vs ColE1) and GFP variant (stable GFP-mut3 vs unstable GFP-ASV) on signal distribution. Heat shock experiments showed that pBBR1 with GFP-ASV gave better dynamic range and sensitivity. Validated system with five proteins: PARP1-BRCT, BRCA1-BRCT, p19, E6, and a control. Observed that BRCA1-BRCT and E6 were insoluble, with different GFP levels. Tested NusA and SUMO solubility tags; they improved solubility and reduced GFP signal. Applied system for deep mutational scanning of PARP1-BRCT; sorted cells into bins based on mCherry/
...[clipped]...
process_signals: Translation sensor design: coupling cassette with secondary structure ensures mCherry only expressed upon correct translation
Folding sensor design: leverages natural heat shock response; RpoH release upon misfolding induces IbpA-GFP
Optimization of folding sensor: tested plasmid copy number and GFP stability; pBBR1 with unstable GFP-ASV chosen for better sensitivity
Validation with five proteins showed expected patterns: soluble proteins gave high mCherry, low GFP; insoluble gave low mCherry, high GFP
Solubility tags (NusA, SUMO) improved folding as expected, reducing GFP signal
Deep mutational scanning: used FACS to sort variants by mCherry/GFP ratio; NGS revealed enrichment of stable variants
Correlation between experimental and computational stability predictions validated the system
Mutational data improved protein structure calculations
methods: Next generation sequencing of sorted bins
metrics: Correlation between experimental and predicted ΔΔG
failures_or_limits: BRCA1-BRCT and E6 were insoluble, but with different GFP levels, suggesting dependence on DnaK/DnaJ binding sites
Some variants showed low mCherry but high GFP, indicating misfolding without aggregation?
System may not detect misfolded proteins that do not expose DnaK binding sites

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: The dual-reporter system enables high-throughput screening for translation and folding. It can be used to optimize expression conditions, test solubility tags, and perform deep mutational scanning. The small hexahistidine tag avoids interference with protein structure. The folding sensor relies on RpoH release upon DnaK binding to misfolded proteins; thus, GFP signal intensity depends on the number of DnaK/DnaJ binding sites. BRCA1-BRCT and E6 were insoluble but showed different GFP levels, possibly due to different DnaK binding site exposure. The system can be used with or without the translation sensor. Limitations include dependence on DnaK binding for folding signal, and potential false negatives if misfolded proteins do not expose binding sites. Future directions include using the system for directed evolution and synthetic biology troubleshooting.
process_signals: System validated for multiple applications: condition optimization, tag testing, deep mutational scanning
Folding sensor signal depends on DnaK/DnaJ binding sites; may not detect all misfolded proteins
Different GFP levels for insoluble proteins suggest variable DnaK binding site exposure
System can be used modularly (folding sensor alone or with translation sensor)
failures_or_limits: GFP signal depends on presence of DnaK/DnaJ binding sites in misfolded protein
May not detect misfolded proteins that do not expose DnaK binding sites
Different insoluble proteins give different GFP levels, complicating direct comparison

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Describes construction of folding reporter: IbpA promoter fused to GFP-mut3 or GFP-ASV in pSEVA vectors with different origins (pBBR1, ColE1). Translation sensor: target protein fused to translation coupling cassette and mCherry in pET22b. Also constructed NusA and SUMO fusion proteins. Tested impact of plasmid copy number and GFP stability on folding reporter sensitivity: co-transformed folding reporter plasmids with pET22b (empty) or target protein plasmids, induced heat shock or IPTG, measured GFP by flow cytometry. Used E. coli Rosetta2(DE3)pLysS and MG1655(DE3) strains. Flow cytometry settings: BD FACS-Aria, 488 nm laser for GFP, 561 nm for mCherry. Data normalized to background or vector control.
process_signals: Optimization of folding reporter: compared pBBR1 vs ColE1 origins and stable vs unstable GFP
Used heat shock to induce IbpA promoter for initial testing
Co-transformation of two plasmids (translation and folding sensors) for dual-reporter experiments
Used flow cytometry for single-cell fluorescence measurements
methods: PCR and Gibson assembly for cloning

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The researchers hypothesized that a dual-reporter system could simultaneously monitor translation and folding by coupling translation to mCherry expression and folding stress to GFP expression.
They hypothesized that the folding sensor would respond to misfolded proteins via the RpoH-IbpA pathway, and that the signal would correlate with the extent of misfolding.
They hypothesized that the system could be used for high-throughput screening of mutant libraries to identify variants with improved expression and stability.
They hypothesized that computational stability predictions would correlate with experimental data from the dual-reporter system.
uncertainties_to_resolve: How generalizable is the system to proteins that do not expose DnaK/DnaJ binding sites upon misfolding?
Can the system distinguish between different types of misfolding (e.g., aggregation vs. soluble misfolded)?
What is the dynamic range and sensitivity limit of the folding sensor for weakly misfolding proteins?
How does the system perform in other E. coli strains or under different growth conditions?
Can the system be adapted for membrane proteins or proteins requiring secretion?

events.summary: Heterologous protein expression in E. coli often results in misfolding, aggregation, and degradation, and existing screening methods are low-throughput and cannot simultaneously monitor translation and folding at the single-cell level.

Design of a dual-reporter system with a translation sensor (translation-coupling cassette with mCherry) and a folding sensor (IbpA promoter driving GFP) to simultaneously monitor protein translation and folding at the single-cell level.
Testing the effect of plasmid backbone (pBBR1 vs ColE1) and GFP variant (stable GFP-mut3 vs unstable GFP-ASV) on the folding sensor signal distribution using heat shock induction.

chunk_events.notes: The chunk contains the introduction and early results, describing the problem, system design, and initial optimization experiments.

events.summary: Optimization of folding sensor by comparing plasmid origins and GFP variants under heat shock.
ColE1-based folding sensor showed leaky GFP expression and poor differentiation, while pBBR1 with GFP-ASV gave distinct peaks.
Validation of dual-reporter system with two human proteins: soluble PARP1-BRCT and insoluble BRCA1-BRCT.
Tested effect of solubility tags NusA and SUMO on translation and folding of four model proteins.
Folding sensor GFP fluorescence correlated with protein solubility; insoluble proteins gave high GFP, but levels varied.
Design of dual-reporter system for high-throughput screening of mutant libraries by flow cytometry.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed experimental optimization, validation, and application of the dual-reporter system, including quantitative stability correlation and mutant library screening design.

events.summary: A randomly generated mutant library of PARP1-BRCT was created using error-prone PCR to achieve 1-3 amino acid substitutions per protein.
The dual-reporter system was used to screen the mutant library by FACS, sorting cells with high mCherry (correct translation) and high GFP (misfolding).
numbers_or_metrics: upper 1% of cells with highest GFP fluorescence

events.summary: After sorting, the GFP signal shift in the P2 population indicated enrichment of variants with impaired folding.
ROC analysis gave an AUC of 0.61, indicating reasonable but non-perfect correlation between predicted and experimental stabilities.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk describes the deep mutational scanning experiment, FACS sorting, NGS analysis, computational stability predictions, and validation using structural decoys.

events.summary: 20 variants from deep mutational scanning were synthesized and analyzed by flow cytometry and Western blot.
System detects misfolded proteins that would otherwise be characterized as having no expression.
PARP1-BRCT I33N mutant library was sorted by FACS to identify suppressors, but only WT sequences were found.
Only WT sequences found likely due to I33N being a single nucleotide substitution, making reversion probable.
To find more clones with increased stability, a library with higher mutation frequency could be used.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains experimental validation of deep mutational scanning results, analysis of variant behavior, and an attempt to identify stabilizing suppressors, with a noted limitation.

events.summary: Analysis of PARP1-BRCT mutant library sorting results shows that only WT sequences were found after sorting for increased stability, likely due to I33N being a single nucleotide substitution that easily reverts.
Analysis of GFP fluorescence differences between insoluble proteins BRCA1-BRCT and E6 suggests that the number of DnaK binding sites influences GFP signal intensity.
methods_or_tools: Limbo DnaK binding site prediction tool
numbers_or_metrics: four DnaK binding sites for BRCA1-BRCT
two DnaK binding sites for E6

events.summary: Limitation: GFP fluorescence may not be comparable when investigating unrelated proteins because the signal depends on the number of DnaK binding sites.
Validation: For variants of the same protein, GFP output can be used as a direct measure of protein stability, as demonstrated for CI2.
Analysis: The translation sensor differentiates between nonsense and missense mutations because reporter signal is only generated upon complete translation.
Analysis: Silent mutations may improve protein solubility and stability by affecting translational rate and co-translational folding.

Limitation: The dual-reporter system can identify variants with high translation and solubility, but a downstream activity assay is needed to ensure enzyme activity, as mutations near catalytic sites may reduce activity.

Identification of PARP1-BRCT mutants with altered folding properties using FACS.

FACS-based CI2 stability assay to test CI2 mutants with varying stabilities.

It does not introduce new hypotheses or major findings but provides detailed protocols supporting the paper's validation and application.

This chunk contains only boilerplate text about competing interests, supplementary information, correspondence, peer review, reprints, publisher's note, open access license, and an image. No research evolution events are present.

evidence_bank.quote: heterologous expression of recombinant proteins in bacteria such as E. coli, a valued host for industrial expression of a wide range of peptides and proteins, often results in misfolding, aggregation and degradation.
no system enables simultaneous monitoring of translation and folding at the single cell level.
we have developed a dual-reporter system that simultaneously monitors protein translation and protein folding at the single cell level (Fig. 1a).

The translation sensor consists of a translation-coupling cassette comprised of a strong secondary mRNA structure formed by a C-terminal hexa-histidine tag, a stop codon for the gene of interest and a ribosome binding site (RBS) for a downstream fluorescent reporter protein, mCherry.
The protein folding sensor is based on the naturally occurring heat shock response system in E. coli.
In our protein folding sensor, the RpoH inducible IbpA promoter is inserted upstream of a GFP reporter gene
To test whether the copy number affects the lbpAp-GFP activity, we have analyzed the heat shock response from two vector backbones with the pBBR1 or the ColE1 ORI, respectively.
The two ORIs were further tested in combination with two GFP-variants, GFP-mut3 and GFP-ASV.

Monitoring of the heat shock response signals of protein folding sensors (pSEVA441-IbpAp and pSEVA631(Sp)-IbpAp) with different origin of replications (ColE1 and pBBR1, respectively) and GFP variants (GFP-mut3 and GFP-ASV) after induction of the IbpA promoter.
two sharp well-defined peaks were observed from the heat induced and uninduced pBBR1 plasmids with a 3–5-fold increase in the signal-to-noise ratio.

The use of the short half-life of GFP-ASV resulted in a lower basal fluorescence level thereby giving higher signal-to-noise ratios, which enabled the distinction of the heat shock induced response from protein misfolding in single cells.

Overexpression of the insoluble BRCA1-BRCT, however, promoted binding of RpoH to the IbpA promoter region of the folding sensor, resulting in a 5–10-fold increase in GFP-signal compared to PARP1-BRCT.

The N-terminally linked solubility tags did not have a large impact on the translation level of PARP1-BRCT, BRCA1-BRCT, and E6, which all expressed also without the tags, and the SUMO tag even decreased the expression level of BRCA1-BRCT.
Different levels of GFP fluorescence were observed for BRCA1-BRCT and E6 although they both were expressed as insoluble protein.
The results demonstrate that it is possible to combine both biosensors to simultaneously investigate translation and proper folding of proteins in E. coli.
We thus demonstrate the use of the dual-reporter system to screen for variants with either reduced or increased stability in a high-throughput mode.

a randomly generated mutant library of pET22-PARP1-BRCT-mCherry was expressed with the protein folding sensor pSEVA631(Sp)-IbpAp-GFP-ASV to demonstrate the applicability of the dual-reporter system to screen for new variants with correct translation but altered stability.

The mutant library was prepared using the error-prone DNA polymerase Mutazyme II that provides a minimal mutational bias. By adjusting the amount of initial target DNA and the number of gene duplications, a mutation rate of 1–3 amino acid substitutions per protein was achieved.

The cell cultures were sorted using FACS for high mCherry signal (P1) alone and for both high mCherry signal (P1) and high GFP signal (P2). Gate 1 (P1) was defined as a mCherry signal higher than the control plasmid background, to ensure that only cells expressing correctly translated proteins were collected. Gate 2 (P2) was defined as the upper 1% of cells with the highest GFP fluorescence, in order to select for variants expressing proteins with decreased stability.

The PARP1-BRCT library sorted for both high mCherry signal and a high GFP signal (Lib. P2) shows a clear shift in GFP signal compared to PARP1-BRCT WT and the PARP1-BRCT library that was only sorted for correct translation (Lib. P1) (Fig. 5b). The shift in GFP signals indicates that protein variants with impaired folding properties had been enriched.
From this analysis, we obtained an area under the curve of 0.61 suggestive of a reasonable but non-perfect correlation between the calculated stabilities and the experimental sequencing data.
The 20 variants were synthesized and introduced into the translation sensor plasmid and analyzed using flow cytometry, and the protein concentration was quantified using Western blots
the system is capable of detecting misfolded proteins that would otherwise have been characterized as proteins with no expression.
all the selected clones were found to encode wild type PARP1-BRCT, except for one silent mutation, P10P

Our observations that only WT sequences were found after sorting for proteins with increased stability is a likely result of the I33N mutation being a single nucleotide substitution making it likely to revert back to the WT PARP1-BRCT sequence.
To increase the likelihood of finding more clones with increased stability, a library with a higher mutation frequency could be used.

Our observations that only WT sequences were found after sorting for proteins with increased stability is a likely result of the I33N mutation being a single nucleotide substitution making it likely to revert back to the WT PARP1-BRCT sequence.
Assuming that all DnaK binding sites are exposed in the unfolded protein, this may explain the difference in GFP intensity between the two proteins.
This suggests that the GFP fluorescence may not be comparable when investigating unrelated proteins.
For variants of the same protein, the GFP output can be used as a direct measure of high or low protein stability, as we have demonstrated for CI2.

The incorporation of the translation sensor makes it possible to differentiate between nonsense and missense mutations since the reporter signal will only be generated when complete translation of the target protein has been achieved.

Where the amino acid sequence determines the three-dimensional fold of a protein the nucleotide sequence may affect the translational rate and thus co-translational folding of the proteins. Silent mutations may therefore still improve protein solubility and stability.

The dual-reporter system can be used to obtain protein variants with high translation levels and high or moderate solubility; however, a downstream activity assay is needed to ensure an active enzyme.
The impact of the vector copy number and intracellular turnover rate of GFP, respectively, on the protein folding reporter system was analyzed to optimize the readout sensitivity of the assay.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Heterologous protein expression in E. coli often leads to misfolding, aggregation, and degradation. Existing screening methods are low-throughput and cannot simultaneously monitor translation and folding at the single-cell level.

canonical_events.summary: Heterologous protein expression in E. coli often results in misfolding, aggregation, and degradation, and existing screening methods are low-throughput and cannot simultaneously monitor translation and folding at the single-cell level.

Design of a dual-reporter system with a translation sensor (translation-coupling cassette with mCherry) and a folding sensor (IbpA promoter driving GFP) to simultaneously monitor protein translation and folding at the single-cell level.

Optimization of folding sensor by testing plasmid backbone (pBBR1 vs ColE1) and GFP variant (stable GFP-mut3 vs unstable GFP-ASV) using heat shock induction; pBBR1 with GFP-ASV gave better dynamic range and sensitivity.

Validation of dual-reporter system with five proteins (PARP1-BRCT, BRCA1-BRCT, p19, E6, control); soluble proteins gave high mCherry/low GFP, insoluble gave low mCherry/high GFP, but GFP levels varied between insoluble proteins.
key_numbers: 5–10-fold increase in GFP-signal for insoluble BRCA1-BRCT

canonical_events.summary: Testing solubility tags NusA and SUMO; they improved solubility and reduced GFP signal for some proteins, but SUMO decreased expression of BRCA1-BRCT.

Deep mutational scanning of PARP1-BRCT: random mutant library (1-3 mutations per protein) screened by FACS using dual-reporter; sorted cells with high mCherry and high GFP to enrich misfolding variants.
key_numbers: upper 1% of cells with highest GFP fluorescence

canonical_events.summary: After sorting, GFP signal shift indicated enrichment of variants with impaired folding; NGS analysis of sorted bins showed correlation between experimental and computational stability predictions (FoldX, Rosetta) with AUC=0.61.

20 variants from deep mutational scanning were synthesized and analyzed by flow cytometry and Western blot; 14 misfolding and 6 non-interfering variants confirmed expected patterns, validating the system.

Attempt to identify suppressor mutations of PARP1-BRCT I33N by sorting a new mutant library for high translation and low GFP; only WT sequences and one silent mutation (P10P) were found, likely due to reversion of single nucleotide substitution.

Analysis of GFP fluorescence differences between insoluble proteins BRCA1-BRCT and E6 suggests that the number of DnaK binding sites influences GFP signal intensity; Limbo tool predicted four sites for BRCA1-BRCT and two for E6.
key_numbers: four DnaK binding sites for BRCA1-BRCT
two DnaK binding sites for E6

canonical_events.summary: Limitation: GFP fluorescence may not be comparable between unrelated proteins because the signal depends on the number of DnaK binding sites; for variants of the same protein, GFP output can be used as a direct measure of stability.
Validation: The system detects misfolded proteins that would otherwise be characterized as having no expression; translation sensor differentiates nonsense from missense mutations.

Limitation: The dual-reporter system can identify variants with high translation and solubility, but a downstream activity assay is needed to ensure enzyme activity, as mutations near catalytic sites may reduce activity.
Analysis: Silent mutations may improve protein solubility and stability by affecting translational rate and co-translational folding, as seen with PARP1-BRCT P10P.

edges.reason: The problem of low-throughput screening motivates the design of the dual-reporter system.
The design requires optimization of the folding sensor components.
Optimized folding sensor is used to validate the dual-reporter system with model proteins.
After validation, solubility tags are tested to demonstrate system utility.
Validation confirms system readiness for deep mutational scanning.
Sorting results are analyzed by NGS and computational predictions.
Selected variants are individually tested to confirm the screening results.
A specific misfolding variant (I33N) is used to attempt suppressor identification.
Observation of different GFP levels in insoluble proteins leads to analysis of DnaK binding sites.
Analysis of DnaK binding sites reveals a limitation in comparing unrelated proteins.
System capability to detect misfolded proteins is highlighted as a validation.
Validation of variants leads to the recognition that activity assays are still needed.
Identification of a silent mutation prompts analysis of its potential effect on folding.

must_cover_points: Problem of low-throughput screening for protein expression and folding
Design of dual-reporter system with translation and folding sensors
Optimization of folding sensor (plasmid copy number, GFP stability)
NGS analysis and correlation with computational predictions
Attempt to find suppressors of I33N
Analysis of DnaK binding site influence on GFP signal
Limitation: GFP signal not comparable between unrelated proteins
Validation: system detects misfolded proteins and differentiates mutation types
Limitation: need for downstream activity assay
